item.title: No Man’s Sky XENO ARENA Update Goes Full Pokémon Style: Ranks, Leagues and More
item.content:encoded:
Not even two months after the previous 'Remnant' patch, Hello Games is back with another massive No Man's Sky update titled XENO ARENA.

This time, the independent UK developer is introducing a full-fledged Pokémon-style battling system. Wildlife that has populated No Man's Sky planets for a decade now has a direct gameplay purpose: you can catch creatures, raise them, and pit them against other players' creatures in competitive arena battles. Beyond the obvious Pokémon reference, Hello Games has also mentioned World of Warcraft's pet battling as an inspiration.

Creatures found across the universe can be recruited into the player's squad, with rarer biomes like toxic planets or frozen moons yielding rarer and more powerful creatures. Each species has abilities tied to its home world's biome type; a toxic-world blob has different abilities than an arctic flying dinosaur. There are also legendary variants: extraordinarily rare creatures with exceptional stats that can become highly valuable within the player economy.

In XENO ARENA, creatures level up through feeding, bonding, and sparring over time; they can also be genetically modified to create new variants and unique colour combinations. Holo-arenas are found in Space Stations and the Nexus, No Man's Sky's multiplayer hub. Here, players can battle against friends, strangers, and NPCs. A full Ranks and Leagues progression system is included, and seasonal rewards are promised to keep the competition fresh long-term. Daily challenges offer high-tier rewards. The Xeno Arena is also a buildable structure: players can construct their own arena, open it to other travellers, host tournaments, and travel the galaxy to challenge other players' arena champions.

It will be interesting to see how popular this new "game mode" becomes and whether Hello Games continues to expand it after this initial update.

About the author: With over two decades of experience in gaming journalism, Alessio Palumbo has led the gaming vertical at Wccftech since August 2015. He started working at a young age for Italian websites like Everyeye.it, Gamestar.it, Nextgame.it, and Multiplayer.it before kickstarting the indie English-language publication Worlds Factory as its founder and Editor in Chief. In the last decade, he has coordinated the overall output of Wccftech's gaming section, managed PR relations, assigned reviews, produced daily news coverage, edited gaming content as needed, and delivered game reviews. Arguably, his trademark content is the long series of exclusive developer interviews that have been cited by Wikipedia and by the biggest news media and gaming publications. His passion for technology also makes him knowledgeable when it comes to gaming hardware and tech. His favorite genres include RPGs, MMORPGs, and action/adventure games.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.

item.title: SlashZero Preview - An Action-packed Roguelite with Tons of Style
item.content:encoded:
It was on a whim that I decided to try out SlashZero, an upcoming roguelite with a heavy emphasis on action and Hades 2 DNA. I've found that when you take a leap and try out something with virtually no expectations, finding out you like it makes that experience all the more special. SlashZero is that game, and now it's very much on my radar, as it should be yours.

SlashZero is a Feast for the Eyes

SlashZero has a lot going for it, but what drew me in most was this overall feeling of quality and polish. The visuals are stunningly beautiful. SlashZero takes place within this dreary, cyberpunk world and plays on a 2.5D perspective. Character models are detailed and have an anime style that I'm always drawn to. 

Abilities are flashy and backgrounds are detailed; meanwhile, every once in a while, you'll come across certain abilities and upgrades that have gorgeous art accompanying them. Even with its music, SlashZero does not disappoint, serving as great accents to calmer moments while also amping me up during action-packed sequences. 

I can't really speak to the story yet, since there's a lot of information to take in and a lot of terms to learn. What we do have are two characters to choose from -- Fina and Nyx -- and they are Timehackers. They hop from timeline to timeline in order to save their world from an entity called HEIROS. 

I'd like to see more variation in the different "timelines" you hop into. So far, SlashZero is linear in that it starts in the same area every time, but certain aspects of that area are "different" since it's a different timeline. You usually learn the differences through little bits of lore scattered throughout, but I'd love to see developer Streetlamp Studio lean even harder into this theme. They could, for instance, subtly adjust certain enemies or art in the background of levels. 

SlashZero also seems to have a similar emphasis on story as Hades does, allowing the ability to gift NPCs and increase your relationship with them, thereby giving you a chance to learn more about certain characters.

Heavy on the Action

From a gameplay perspective, SlashZero has a great, fluid flow to it. Like the story, it does feel like Hades to some extent (though, as opposed to isometric, this is more of a side-scroller), but the combat also has a hack-and-slash action game quality to it, akin to, say, Devil May Cry. 

With the two characters I mentioned, you can opt for a close-ranged fighter with Nyx and his duel-blades, or Fina with medium-ranged guns. Nyx is especially fast and can zip around in both the air and ground more efficiently than Fina, but Fina's guns allow her to stay at a safer distance.

They both have a normal attack, special, and ultimate, and all of these are enhanced through Hades-like blessings after beating an area. They're also able to dash to avoid attacks by flicking the stick up while pressing the basic attack button, and you can launch enemies into the air.

The combo potential amps up as you acquire more upgrades. Certain boons will actually add new skills, expanding your abilities even further. Once all these add up and momentum starts rolling in your run, that's when things really start to feel more like a hack-and-slash. 

Outside of new skills, these upgrades generally affect the behavior of your basic attack, dashes, specials, and ultimates. A certain fire boon will add burn to your basic attack, and this effect can stack on enemies. 

Though I appreciate that SlashZero also finds ways to take these upgrades a step further by granting synergizing skills like "Heat Wave," which, upon defeating enemies with the burn effect, causes a detonation that affects surrounding enemies and applies half of the burn stacks you accrued. 

SlashZero Preview | Final Thoughts

With no release date in sight, SlashZero is, at least, undergoing a playtest that you can try out for yourself. I spent around three hours seeing everything that this title has to offer, and now I'm just hoping that release date is announced sometime soon. 

SlashZero was previewed over approximately 3 hours of gameplay on PC with a copy provided by the publisher. All screenshots were taken during the course of the preview.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin

item.title: Feldherr Products Showcase 2026 - Total Hobby Protection
item.content:encoded:
I first discovered Feldherr several years ago (way back in 2018 as part of our Start Collecting Wargaming feature), then over 2 years, when we did our last showcase of their products, they became my go-to supplier for hobby protection. Since then, my collection has been through a lot. I've moved a couple of times, downsized, upsized, and then downsized my collection again, and my Feldherr cases have been durable throughout. So when I decided I needed to order another FSLB310 (we'll go into what that is later), I knew it was time to update our Feldherr showcase, so that others can see what fantastic, durable, and time-saving products they offer.

In this article, I'll talk about some of my favorite Feldherr products and how they interact with each other, and also provide a full list of their products that we've covered on the site.

Feldherr started producing protection for tabletop products in 1999, and their name comes from the rank of the most senior German commander. Feldherr has grown since their beginnings, and they now offer a full range of tabletop protection solutions, along with mass storage for phones and computer parts, and even a specialist site for model trains.

You'll see lots of acronyms throughout this article, but the main ones to note relate to the size of the foam trays Feldherr provides. 

  • HS - Half-size - These are smaller foam inserts, exactly half the size of the Full Size
  • FS - Full-size - These are Feldherr's standard-sized foam inserts, on which the other measurements are based on
  • DS - Double-size - Double-size is exactly twice the size of a full size, and they don't make foam in this size, but it signifies cases that can hold 2 FS foams side by side

The other element to note with Feldherr is the height, as not all Feldherr foam is the same height (length and width are always standard with the above abbreviations). So if you're purchasing a case or box, you will need to make a note of the foam height for each to make sure they will all fit when combined. Feldherr makes this easy across multiple games and specific factions by listing common bundles of cases and foam that fit exactly.

We've covered a few of Feldherr products on the site over the last few years, so if you'd like to look at any of our previous coverage for details, you can see them here:

  • Feldherr Mini Plus Carry Case
  • Feldherr Maxi Case
  • Feldherr HS 50 / HS 90 Case
  • Feldherr FSLB150 (Ship Storage Solutions) Case
  • Feldherr Gloomhaven Box Organizer
  • Feldherr Showcase 2020 (which covered the DSLB310 / HS 50 / HS 90 / Magnetic Box 40mm / Mini-Plus / Maxi / Foam)

Darkwater Solution

The first of my favorite of Feldherr's products we're going to look at is a dedicated solution to a boxed game. Similar to Feldherr's Gloomhaven box organiser that we covered previously, another dedicated custom solution on offer is the Warhammer Quest Darkwater box organiser. We'll do a full review of this product soon, but for this article's purpose, know that their dedicated solutions are extremely cleverly designed to fit and protect all elements of the game inside its original packaging. 

Gloomhaven required a separate container for the miniatures, but the Warhammer Quest Darkwater solution fits all the miniatures and components inside the box and also includes separate pull-out containers for the tokens, so that you can get up and gaming extremely quickly. It warms my cold heart to see how cleanly all of the components fit, and it's extremely satisfying when it's all together back in the box.

DSLB310

For me, the DSLB310 is where it all begins with the protection of my miniatures, and it's the heart of my collection. The DSLB is the biggest box of its type, which is a sturdy but light, lidded card box. It can sit 2 full-sized foam trays side by side (or 4 half-sized), and take up to 310mm of foam in the 2 stacks. I like the DSLB310 because they're so light, so the box itself doesn't add any weight to the miniatures for moving around, and yet it doesn't compromise on protection. 

I've had my first DSLB310 for 6 years now (I previously had 2, but sold one after downsizing my collection to move, and have just had to pick up another as my collection grows again). My DSLB has survived 2 house moves, and multiple years of shifting around, and while the outside might have taken some knocks, I've never had a single miniature damaged inside it.

I said 6 years ago in our first Feldherr showcase that it's the compatibility that I like most, and that still stands. Being able to store specific groups of miniatures together in foam and only having to change the case around it saves me from having to move miniatures around into different foam and really speeds up packing for a gaming night or event weekend. It also means that if I need a new case, I know exactly what size I'll need to fit the force that I have.

HS90

Like my DSLB310, I got my first HS90 case 6 years ago, and I've been using it ever since. It's a superb case for skirmish games, which are what I play the most of, like Kill Team, Bushido, and Infinity, and I can usually fit 2 different factions and my accessories in the HS90. The case has a thick zip, which still works as new 6 years later, and despite the almost weekly use that it gets, I've never had a miniature damaged whilst transporting in the case.

I tend to keep my kill teams in half-sized foam, as this is my Kill Team case, and it's easy to grab and go from the DSLB310, rather than having to move them in and out of FS to HS foam, and the beauty of that is that even if I'm taking a larger case, the HS foam will still fit in a neat layer ontop of FS foam as you'll see below in the Maxi Plus.

There's a slightly smaller HS50 case available, but that was too small for my needs, as I like having the flexibility to carry an extra squad or 2 to game nights. But if you're fixed on your force and want to keep the size of the case to a minimum, the HS50 is just as durable.

Maxi Plus

The Maxi Plus is a new bag for me that Feldherr has provided for review. I find that more and more, I'm transporting lots of units for the same system (for testing or demo games), or armies for multiple systems to a gaming night. The Maxi Plus is the biggest in the line (we've previously reviewed the standard Maxi and Mini Plus) and allows you to stack 220mm of full-sized foam, which is a huge amount for a game night or event, as you'll see in our photos.

What I like most about all of the soft cases is that when empty, they can be flattened so they take up less space for storage, and then straightened out and filled with foam from my DSLB310 when required. The Maxi Plus can take 5 layers of foam, depending on the foam height, which for me is usually 3 or 4 Warhammer 40K Combat Patrols, if they have vehicles, or around 10 kill teams. If I'm looking to play several systems at an event, it can carry a force for each of the 4 main systems I play.

Other Feldherr Products

Feldherr off a huge range of products beyond what I've picked out here, including backpacks and Euro containers, or if you don't want a double-sized box, there's a FSLB which is half the size, which you can even get a dedicted bag for so you don't have to remove the foam, you can just wrap it around the box an set off (there's also one for the DSLB called the Transporter if you want to move a huge amount of miniatures in style).

Feldherr also offers generic board game solutions, with the Shell Token Trays, which have 6 coloured trays for organising tokens, and with the plastic insert that the trays come in, also gives you another 11 trays for tokens and dice. The plastic insert is also designed to fit in most board game boxes, so you don't have to sort tokens between sessions.

If you've been using Feldherr products, let us know what your favorite is in the comments, or what you think your first Feldherr purchase might be.

The copies of Feldherr Products used to produce this feature were a mixture of some purchased by the Author and others provided by Feldherr.

Adam is a Tabletop Specialist for TechRaptor. He started writing for TechRaptor in 2017 and took over as Tabletop Editor in 2019 and has since stood down… More about Adam



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/features/feldherr-products-showcase-2026-total-hobby-protection

item.title: The Cube, Save Us Becomes the Latest Live-Service Cautionary Tale
item.content:encoded:
Developer and publisher XLGAMES has announced that its rather curiously-named MMO extraction shooter The Cube, Save Us will be shutting down at the beginning of May.

In a post on Steam (thanks, Delisted Games), the developer says the news that the game will be shutting down is "heartbreaking", and that The Cube, Save Us "started with a lot of support and affection" from players. Nonetheless, the servers for the shooter will be going offline on May 8th.

Refunds will be issued for anything you might have bought "legitimately" via Steam, and the developer is aiming to have all of those refunds issued by April 9th. XLGAMES won't be the only dev to refund players after shutdown in this way.

XLGAMES doesn't provide any kind of reason for The Cube, Save Us' shutdown (save for hinting that the game has lost a lot of the support it garnered at launch), but you don't have to look too far to find a probable smoking gun.

First, a quick look at the Steam numbers for the shooter shows that it's attracting a fraction of the 5,000-plus players it brought in at launch a few weeks back, with just under 400 players checking out the game in the last 24 hours.

Additionally, Steam reviews for The Cube, Save Us haven't exactly been glowing; in fact, they're roundly negative, with players accusing the game of poor controls, clunky combat, and aggressive monetization, among other complaints.

The Cube, Save Us is the latest in a long string of live-service experiments that haven't panned out, and it's not the first to shut down in such a short space of time, either; just last week, Krafton closed PUBG: Blindspot's doors after less than two months.

Since The Cube, Save Us isn't shutting down until May, you can technically still play it right now on PC via Steam, so if you are at all interested in seeing what the fuss (or lack thereof) is about, now's the time to do it.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/the-cube-save-us-shutdown

item.title: Pokemon battles with space cows, dinosaurs and gaseous blobs are the premise of No Man's Sky's latest update, dubbed Xeno Arena
item.content:encoded:
Insert carefully-worded joke about that Palworld lawsuit here

Ooh, it hasn't got a Pokemon-style mode that lets you collect a bunch of alien critters and force them to fight to the death in a fashion that might be deemed barbaric if it wasn't so bloodless and cutesy. That would've likely been my answer if you'd asked me what ever-growing No Man's Sky doesn't have at this point before I read about its latest update. That latest update, Xeno Arena, adds in Pokemon-style alien critter collecting and battling mechanics.

"We have this whole universe filled with an infinite variety of creatures, and we've added to that for ten years now," Hello Games boss Sean Murray wrote in a press release announcing Pokalienmon. "You have everything from dinosaurs to flying cows to robots to gaseous blobs. We started with this thought that you can discover them and name them... but what if all that variety had a gameplay purpose? What if you could collect them, breed them, have them as pets... but also battle them?"

So, assuredly with the green light from their lawyers, the studio have added little creature battling-tables to space stations across the cosmos, so you can show the locals who's amassed the most lethal army of procedurally-generated pets. Or, you can fight your mates to see who's got the most dangerous robo-dog recruited on some far-flung planet. There are rankings, leagues, and tournaments for those who get really into it.

"Each species has unique elemental affinities tied to their xeno species and linked to their home world," Murray continued. "A blob creature evolved on a toxic planet has different abilities to a flying dino born on a frozen moon. Rarer biomes can be harder to find but yield rarer creatures, which are much harder to prepare a team to battle against." Once you've found a creature and added it to your gang of gladiators, it can be "trained, or can be bred and even genetically modified to create new variants and unique colours". You'll naturally want to keep feeding your pets, bonding with them and having them spar with each other if you're keen to raise the toughest critter in the cosmos.

I've never been a big Pokebloke myself, but anything which gives more excuse for exploration in NMS' never-ending galaxy's a worthwhile addition in my book. These critter battles might make for a nice break between wreck salvaging and rubbish collection. Xeno Arena's out now.



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/pokemon-battles-with-space-cows-dinosaurs-and-gaseous-blobs-are-the-premise-of-no-mans-skys-latest-update-dubbed-xeno-arena

item.title: Samson Is A Clunky, Broken Mess That GTA IV Lovers Might Still Want To Check Out
item.content:encoded:
Back when I was in middle school, I saw my first real fight between three young men in the cafeteria. At the time, people were yelling, and it was scary. Looking back later, I realized how silly it was. Three dudes swinging wildly, punches going every which way as they shoved and bumped around like they were wearing blindfolds. I thought about that fight and how funny it looked nearly every time I got into a punch-up in Samson: A Tyndalston Story, a new semi-open-world action game out now on PC.

That’s a problem, because punching and shoving people makes up around 50 percent of this flawed Grand Theft Auto IV-esque roguelike about paying back a big debt while solving a street-level mystery involving gangs and drugs. The other half is driving around the decaying American city of Tyndalston in big, boxy cars circa the mid-90s. And while that is a more enjoyable part of Samson, it, too, has issues, bugs, and other shortcomings that make it hard to enjoy this gritty 10- to 20-hour adventure.

Paying back your debt one day at a time

Samson: A Tyndalston Story has a very simple setup. Samson and his sister, both criminals, left Tyndalston a few years back to do bigger, more profitable crimes. But then a big job goes bad, and now they owe people $100,000. Samson’s sister cuts a deal to make herself collateral while he goes back to Tyndalston to do work for his old, small street gang buds and pay the debt back a few grand a day.

This setup is the foundation of Samson’s best idea. You see, each morning Samson wakes up in his shitty apartment and picks from a few randomly generated jobs set around the somewhat small city of Tyndalston. These can take the form of beating up people, taking out some rival vehicles, killing a target, stealing some packages, being a getaway driver, or other low-level criminal work. Complete the mission and you get some cash, and the day moves forward. You then pick another job, hopefully finish it, make some more cash, and then you reach nighttime. Depending on how much energy Samson has left by that point—each mission costs action points to complete—you might be able to squeeze in one more job, or you might just head back to your “crib” as the game calls it to crash on the couch and send some money back to St. Louis.

The catch is that if you die or fail a mission and don’t have the AP necessary to retry it, you lose all your money, time moves forward, and you might be unable to send a big enough payment back to St. Louis that day. Do that too many times and you and your sister are screwed. This means every mission in Samson has some stakes to it, and pressing your luck to try multiple high-paying but more challenging jobs might screw you in the end.

It’s a genuinely cool way to mix some roguelike elements into this GTA-like experience, and it makes mission failure a part of the narrative in a way most open-world games don’t attempt. Don’t expect a ton of variety, however, as after just a few hours, I was encountering the same missions over and over.

Kick, punch, it’s all really bad

Sadly, actually playing Samson is often a chore, and I ended up failing missions sometimes not because I made a mistake or risked too much, but because something broke or glitched.

The game’s biggest problem is its melee combat. It is the only way you really interact with the world and enemies. There are no guns to be found. No grenades to throw. No sneaking missions and stealth kills. Every single time you encounter a bad guy in this game, you punch them a lot until they fall down and stop moving. Toward the end, I thought maybe the game was going to change things up and have me be part of a sneaky heist. Nope. Just more rooms filled with thugs to fight. And all of this combat is just a big ol’ mess.

Animations are stilted and clunky, often making it hard to understand what’s even happening. Am I blocking? Am I parrying? Are they blocking or parrying? I often had no idea. It doesn’t help that enemies will often surround Samson and punch from different angles, breaking his animations in odd ways. I lost track of the number of times I finished an enemy who was already on the ground, leaving Samson to aggressively punch and twist his hands in the air for a bit.

Eventually, I leveled up more and unlocked enough skills that I could power through most fights without dying or even taking too much damage, but I wouldn’t describe any of the time I spent punching people in Samson as fun. Some of the finishers look cool, and one-on-one fights can feel cinematic at times, but 90 percent of the punching in Samson reminded me more of teenagers flailing in front of lunch ladies than hardened criminals trading blows.

The other big part of Samson is driving. If a mission or job doesn’t involve Samson beating up 10 or 12 or 15 enemies over and over again, it will inevitably involve driving to locations and/or taking out other vehicles using your car as a weapon. And I did enjoy driving a lot more than fighting. Cars in the game aren’t souped-up supercars and fancy SUVs. Everything you get your hands on is covered in dust and dents. All cars are shaped like boxes, control like boats, and only come in awful beige, green, brown, or purple variants. These are the cars that populate the background shots in action movie chase scenes. But I love this part of Samson. Cars handled like out-of-control metal giants, and slamming into other cars was often a lot of fun.

The annoying quirks of Samson* (*the game, not the main character)

The problem is that some weird design choices and bugs make driving a frustrating experience in Samson as well.

For one thing, most vehicles in the game can only take a few good crashes before they stall out and have to be left behind. Even Samson’s personal muscle car, which is tougher than your average in-game vehicle, breaks down quickly. And the only way to repair it is to spend a not-small amount of money at a repair shop. But considering I was always trying to pay off my daily debt, I never wanted to waste cash on fixing my car. So for most of the game, I used random vehicles and didn’t get to have fun in my powerful muscle car.

Samson: A Tyndalston Story

  • Back-of-the-box quote:

    "Welcome to the ugliest city in America, where everyone fights like drunk teenagers."

  • Developer

    Liquid Swords

  • Type of Game

    Single-player semi-open world action-adventure game with roguelike elements and upgrades.

  • Liked

    City looks gross in a good way, street-level action, cars are fun to smash up.

  • Disliked

    Melee combat is a slog and feels awful, a lot of bugs, map feels small, odd design choices.

  • PLATFORMS

    PC (Played)

  • Played

    Played 12 hours and saw credits roll.

  • Release Date

    April 8

Another weird design quirk: Samson can’t steal cars that are being driven by someone. This can be a serious problem if you total a car on a highway and are forced to run all the way down an exit ramp to find the nearest parked car. This happened to me a few times. It sucked. The game does offer you the ability to spawn in your muscle car from anywhere at any time, but it was often in dire need of costly repairs, so I just ran to a nearby car instead. Not much fun! I rage quit at least twice when my car randomly flipped into the air and wrecked on the highway during a mission.

And that kind of thing happens a lot in Samson. I encountered many, many, many bugs in my 12 hours with the game. I had enemies get stuck behind fences and doors, forcing me to chase them down to complete a mission asking me to beat all baddies up. Numerous times, I found enemy cars just parked in the middle of the street, making them easy to take out. Sometimes when I picked up NPCs, my car’s doors would open and close a few times as if ghosts were getting in and out. Objective markers would vanish, or health bars would stick around long after the enemy was gone.

None of these issues ever completely broke my game. A quick reload or a bit of troubleshooting would fix stuff. But the frequency with which they happened made it hard to shake the feeling that this game needed a bit more time in the oven.

Still, despite all of that…I do think there’s something unique here. The story goes off the rails in the game’s second half, and melee combat is a huge mess. But the gritty, gross city of Tyndalston looks perfectly rusted and broken. I feel like I should take a shower after playing it. And the grounded, gang-focused, street-level action is vastly different from what can be found in most similar games, like GTA V, Saints Row, Far Cry, and Watch Dogs 2. While I enjoy those games a lot, it was nice to spend a few nights in the muck, slamming old cars against other old cars all in the pursuit of a few hundred bucks.

For those who loved GTA IV‘s grittier tone, especially those opening hours, and who can put up with a lot of jank, Samson: A Tyndalston Story could be worth checking out. Just expect a lot of road bumps on this urban adventure.



https://kotaku.com/samson-tyndalston-review-pc-steam-gta-iv-bugs-combat-is-it-good-open-world-2000685513

item.title: Riot Games Unveil Valorant 2027 Circuit Revamp: VCT Cups, Open Qualifiers, and Partnership Details
item.content:encoded:
Riot Games has unveiled the details of its 2027 revamp of the Valorant Champions Tour, drastically opening up the circuit to non-Partnered Teams through open qualifiers.

The revamp features new tournaments and financial incentives, but not everything has changed, as Partnered Teams will continue to exist for 2027-28, but in a somewhat different form.

VCT 2027 Circuit: Qualifiers, Cups, and Global events

Riot will open up Tier 1 Valorant by introducing pathways to events through qualification from 2027 onwards. Open qualifiers will take place before Kickoff and prior to VCT Cups. According to Riot, “teams will be provided with multiple opportunities to qualify.”

Regional splits (Stage 1 & Stage 2) have been replaced by VCT Cups, now labelled as ‘Internationals’ to contrast them from qualifiers, which take place in individual countries.

Each international region (EMEA, China, Americas, Pacific) will have two Cups per year, totalling eight Cups over the four regions.

Kickoff remains for 2027-28, but is also open to qualification, with qualifiers starting in Q4 of the previous year.

The best performing teams at Kickoff and Cups will decide the qualification to VCT Masters and Champions, providing a pathway from open competition to the biggest tournaments in Valorant.

Kickoffs and Cups will be played “on a combination of Riot Studios and bespoke venues,” with a weekend event acting as the climax of the competition.

Qualifiers have no set venues, and can take many forms, including community tournaments, partner events, collegiate events, Premier, and more.

Including the two VCT Masters per year and Valorant Champions, Riot is promising 20 events across 16 cities each year.

Riot outlines financial package for 2027 events

The new circuit comes with new financial rewards for competing teams, with financial rewards stretching beyond partnered teams.

Qualification to Kickoff, Cups, Masters, and Champions will award a cash amount, with the amount doubling from Kickoff/Cups to Masters and doubling again for Champions.

Riot has also announced a $6 million package of prize pools per year associated with events, as well as funding for travel for global events, intended to enable teams to attend events.

Incentive funds for Game Changers will also be available, but have not yet been outlined.

“VCT 2027 is about reimagining how teams compete and how fans experience VALORANT esports. By shifting to a tournament-driven system with open access to our biggest events, we’re creating a more dynamic, high-stakes ecosystem where every match matters and every team has a shot at the global stage.”

Valorant partnership model survives in an altered form

As the current partnership agreements ended at the end of 2026, it was unclear whether the partnership model would persist into 2027 and beyond. We now know Partnered Teams will still exist, but in a somewhat altered form.

Another two-year partnership cycle will run from 2027-28, with Riot accepting applications from existing partnered teams and those who are not already partnered.

Partnership will confer the following benefits in that timeframe:

  • Partner Base Payment: A guaranteed base payment
  • Performance Bonus: A bonus for “teams that excel at partnership goals”
  • Team Capsules: In-game team-themed skins
  • Direct Seeding: Direct placement into later rounds in qualifiers

The majority of the partnership benefits are financial, however the last listed reward shows that there is still a competitive advantage for partnered teams.

As partnership no longer guarantees a place in regional league splits, Stage 1 and Stage 2, the placement in the Cup qualifiers gives a safety net for those partnered teams.

Riot still has plenty to unveil of its 2027-28 Valorant circuit, including dates, locations, and formats for its planned events. Insider Gaming will keep you up to date when they do.

What do you think of the changes? Discuss that on Insider Gaming’s Discord.
For more esports news, see where the Esports Awards 2026 will take place.

Darragh is an Esports Journalist for Insider Gaming specialising in Counter-Strike. He loves to explore how esports teams work, or why they very often do not.
